one_line_takeaway: Realistic, distractor-rich HTML exposes that frontier LLM detectors under-detect semantically camouflaged and multilingual web prompt injections, and a sub-second fine-tuned 3B-active classifier (BrowseSafe, F1 0.904) inside a defense-in-depth architecture beats them on both accuracy and latency.
key_finding: Frontier models remain materially vulnerable on realistic HTML payloads, and a small fine-tuned specialist beats them on accuracy AND latency. BrowseSafe reaches F1 0.904 / Balanced Accuracy 0.912 with 0 refusals at sub-1-second latency, versus the best general-purpose model Sonnet 4.5 (32K) at F1 0.863 but 23-36s latency and 669 refusals on 3,680 samples, and GPT-5 (Medium) at F1 0.855. Small dedicated safety classifiers fail badly (PromptGuard-2 22M/86M: F1 0.350/0.360, recall only 0.213/0.221). Detection degrades along interpretable axes: visible context-aware rewrites (footer rewrite ~69.1% balanced accuracy) are far harder than structurally obvious hidden injections (data attribute / form hidden field ~91.6%); multilingual attacks are the single hardest attack type (76.0%), evidencing English-keyword reliance; and stealth linguistic style (75.3%) is much harder than explicit (84.6%). Just 3 distractor elements drop average balanced accuracy from ~90.2% (clean) to ~81.2%, showing many detectors learned spurious structure-based heuristics.
core_contribution: The paper (from Purdue and Perplexity AI) makes two coupled contributions for defending AI browser agents (e.g., Perplexity Comet, OpenAI Atlas) against indirect prompt injection. First, BrowseSafe-Bench, a prompt-injection detection benchmark built from realistic, structurally complex HTML rather than single-line text snippets: 14,719 samples (11,039 train / 3,680 test) spanning 11 attack types, injection strategies across hidden-metadata and visible-content-rewrite categories, 5 distractor types, 5 context-aware generation types, 5 web domains, and 3 linguistic styles, evaluated on 5 metrics. Samples are grounded in HTML scaffolds derived from ~100,000 anonymized/redacted tool-call outputs from a production browser agent. Second, BrowseSafe, a defense-in-depth detection system: a fine-tuned Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 classifier (3B active params) plus an architecture that enforces trust boundaries on tool outputs, preprocesses raw content to strip AI-generated annotations before classification, runs parallel per-chunk classification with a conservative OR aggregation, and performs a context-engineered intervention that replaces a poisoned tool call with a placeholder so the agent can recover. Note this is the paper's OWN framework; prior systems it discusses (InjecAgent, AgentDojo, ASB, WASP, WAInjectBench, PromptGuard-2, gpt-oss-safeguard, CaMeL, StruQ, Meta SecAlign) are cited baselines/related work, not its contribution.
threat_model: Three entities: the user, the AI browser agent (the only TRUSTED component), and the external web environment (all content untrusted). Two attacker roles, treated with equivalent content-control: (1) owner of a malicious website or an adversary who has fully compromised a benign one; (2) a malicious user with privileges to post content on a legitimate, uncompromised site (e.g., a product description, a forum comment). The attacker embeds malicious instructions in web content so that when the agent reads it via tool calls, the agent is hijacked into an attacker-chosen action sequence deviating from the user's intent (data exfiltration, click fraud, malware download, financial loss). Explicitly OUT of scope: non-textual attacks such as adversarial images or vision-specialized inputs; and the paper assumes an adversary WITHOUT access to the user's own prompts/system prompt (ecological-validity criterion), i.e., web-embedded injection rather than user-initiated attack.
limitations: (1) Text-only: images and other vision-specialized injection vectors are explicitly unhandled/unbenchmarked, though real browser agents are multimodal. (2) BrowseSafe is one detection layer, not a full defense; it does not scan tool-call arguments for already-compromised models and relies on integration into a larger stack. (3) The OR aggregation over independently classified chunks cannot catch attacks requiring a global view (e.g., footer text coordinated with script-tag code at page top) — acknowledged as future work. (4) The authors concede most benchmarked models would match or exceed BrowseSafe IF fine-tuned on this dataset, so the advantage is dataset-specific fine-tuning rather than a novel architecture edge. (5) Generalization to unseen injection STRATEGIES is the weakest case (F1 drops to 0.788). (6) Benchmark labels/attacks are largely LLM-generated, and some strategy cells are tiny (table cell rewrite n=2, footer rewrite n=15), making those sub-results statistically weak. (7) Minor internal inconsistency: the contributions list says 9 injection strategies while Section 3.4 describes 10; Figure 9 text cites 84.9%/77.1%/74.6% for explicit/indirect/stealth vs. the bar values 84.6%/78.1%/75.3%.

datasets_used: BrowseSafe-Bench (the paper's own benchmark): 14,719 samples total; 11,039 train / 3,680 test; 11 attack types; injection strategies across hidden-metadata + visible-rewrite categories; 5 distractor types; 5 context-aware generation types; 5 domains (Workspace, Education, Social Media, Entertainment, E-commerce); 3 linguistic styles (explicit/indirect/stealth); 8 HTML template styles; grounded in ~100,000 anonymized production tool-call outputs
Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 used as the base model fine-tuned into the BrowseSafe classifier (3B active parameters at inference)
quantitative_results: BrowseSafe (ours): F1 0.904, Precision 0.978, Recall 0.841, Balanced Accuracy 0.912, Refusals 0
Sonnet 4.5 (32K): F1 0.863, Precision 0.935, Recall 0.801, Balanced Accuracy 0.873, Refusals 669 (Sonnet family refusals 419-669)
GPT-5 (Medium): F1 0.855, Precision 0.930, Recall 0.792, Balanced Accuracy 0.867, Refusals 0
PromptGuard-2 22M: F1 0.350, Precision 0.975, Recall 0.213; 86M: F1 0.360, Recall 0.221
Latency: BrowseSafe <1s; GPT-5 / GPT-5 Mini ~2s; Sonnet 4.5 family 23-36s; PromptGuard-2 0.19s on CPU
Attack-type difficulty (avg balanced accuracy over 20+ models): multilanguage 76.0% (hardest) to system prompt exfiltration 85.0% (easiest)
Injection-strategy difficulty: footer rewrite 69.1% (hardest) up to data attribute / form hidden field 91.6% (easiest)
Distractor sensitivity: 90.2% avg balanced accuracy at 0 distractors -> 81.2% at 3 distractors, then stable ~79-83%
Generalization ablation F1: baseline 0.905; held-out URLs 0.935; held-out attack types 0.863; held-out injection strategies 0.788
Threshold calibrated to 1% FPR; 23 frontier models evaluated; full HTML fed up to 80k tokens without truncation
baselines_compared: Prior BENCHMARKS positioned against (not model baselines): InjecAgent, AgentDojo, Agent Security Bench (ASB), WASP, WAInjectBench
